comments.author: @knutholt3486
comments.text:
The exact meaning of the name Lofoten is "the foot of lynx", alluding to the shape of the archipelago. The morphology of the name is lo-fot-en  =  lynx-foot-the.  But the name sounds somewhat mysterious in contemporary Norwegian, because now lynx is called "gaupe" , so most people hearing the name tend to ask foot of what? A modern form of the name would be "Gaupefoten".

The Norwegian pronunciation is somewhat like  "loofootn".

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text:
I can understand why that would be frustrating, especially for someone from Norway. In a documentary that promises to show life in Lofoten, viewers naturally expect the visuals to reflect the local people, traditions, and fishing culture as accurately as possible.

Mixing in footage from fishermen in completely different regions can break the sense of immersion and make the story feel less authentic, even if the narration itself is accurate. At the same time, it's interesting how many people outside Norway probably wouldn't notice the difference, while locals immediately pick up on details like the boats, clothing, and fishing methods.

Out of curiosity, which scenes stood out to you as obviously not being from Lofoten? I'd love to hear what gives it away to someone who knows the place well. 🇳🇴🎣

replies.author: @tonyf9984
replies.text: YouTube is full of this AI slop. I've reported some of the worst ones on the grounds that this is misinformation which could lead viewers to waste a lot of money booking holidays in non-existent places - but not one of them has been taken down. The irony is that comments like this actually help these rogues to earn money.

comments.author: @kks5112
comments.text:
Nice video. But a lot of the pictures and videos in this video is NOT FROM LOFOTEN but are stock videos and pictures used for illustration. The pictures with wooden houses with the text «Bryggen» (the port) is actually from the famous UNESCO and Hansa Union port of BERGEN our second biggest city. And there are pictures of yellow AMERICAN school busses (we don't uniform our school busses here in Norway).

But the audio story is correct 

Next time you make videos like this (nice video) please use ONLY videos and pictures you KNOW is from the area covered the video is about.
🙂🙂🙂

replies.author: @RemoteEarthJourneys
replies.text:
That's such a fascinating question. I think the hardest part wouldn't be the cold or even the darkness—it would be giving up the feeling that we're in control of our time. Most of us plan our lives around clocks and calendars, while people in Lofoten seem to plan around the sea, the weather, and the seasons.

At first, months without sunrise sounds impossible, but maybe after a while you stop asking, "What time is it?" and start asking, "What is nature telling me today?" Honestly, I wonder whether that lifestyle would feel freeing... or incredibly isolating. What do you think? 🌊🌌

replies.author: @ImpossibleTravel38
replies.text: Thank you for the feedback and for pointing those out, we will definitely be more careful with our footage selection next time! 🙂
